summary: Oda Nobunaga was the daimyo of Owari Province who began the unification of Japan during the Sengoku period, ending it as ruler of roughly half the country before his 1582 death; he was followed as unifier by his general Toyotomi Hideyoshi and then Tokugawa Ieyasu. Clues center on his upset victory over Imagawa Yoshimoto at the Battle of Okehazama, his destruction of the Buddhist warrior-monks of Mount Hiei and the Ikko-ikki, his revolutionary use of massed arquebusiers in three ranks to crush the Takeda clan's cavalry at the Battle of Nagashino, and his betrayal and forced suicide by his retainer Akechi Mitsuhide in the Honno-ji Incident.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Nobunaga is identified in giveaways as the daimyo of Owari Province and son of Nobuhide who became the first of Japan's "Three Great Unifiers," beginning the unification of Japan during the Sengoku period before being succeeded by Toyotomi Hideyoshi and then Tokugawa Ieyasu.

clues.clue: First of Japan's "Three Great Unifiers," succeeded by Toyotomi Hideyoshi and then Tokugawa Ieyasu
examples: name this unifier of Japan who was succeeded by Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Tokugawa Ieyasu.
For 10 points, name this first great unifier of Japan.

clues.clue: Son of Nobuhide; daimyo/ruler of Owari Province during the Sengoku Jidai
examples: name this son of Nobuhide and predecessor to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, the first of the great unifiers of Japan.
Name this Owari warlord, the first of three great unifiers of Japan during the Sengoku Jidai.

clues.clue: Overthrew the Ashikaga shogunate and united roughly half of Japan, paving the way for his ally Tokugawa Ieyasu to found his own shogunate
examples: name this man who overthrew the Ashikaga shogunate and united half of Japan, paving the way for his ally, Tokugawa Ieyasu, to found his own shogunate.

works.name: Early Life and Rise to Power
works.description: Nobunaga was initially disfavored in the succession struggle following his father's death for his poor conduct, but consolidated control of his clan through a mix of family reconciliation, betrayal, and ruthlessness before expanding beyond Owari.

clues.clue: Was initially disfavored in the succession struggle after his father Nobuhide's death because he dressed badly and threw incense at the funeral, prompting a retainer's suicide
examples: This man was initially disfavored in the succession struggle resulting from his father's death, because he dressed badly and threw incense at the funeral.
One of this man's retainers committed suicide after this man threw incense at the altar during his father's funeral.

clues.clue: Defeated his brother Nobuyuki at the Battle of Ino but was persuaded by their mother to pardon him and his retainers, before later faking illness to have Nobuyuki killed at Kiyosu Castle on information from retainer Shibata Katsuie
examples: This man defeated his brother at the Battle of Ino, but was persuaded by his mother to pardon that brother and his retainers.
He later faked an illness to kill that brother in Kiyosu Castle on the information of one of those retainers, Shibata Katsuie, who would become one of this leader's right-hand men.

clues.clue: Eliminated a rival warlord by sending an assassin to lie in wait in the rival's latrine and stab him with a spear
examples: It didn't happen in England, but this man once eliminated a rival warlord by sending an assassin to lay in wait in his enemy's latrine and stab him with a spear while he was relieving himself.

clues.clue: Nearly killed when an Azai clan samurai charged his tent at the Battle of Anegawa
examples: This first unifier of Japan almost died when an Azai clan samurai charged his tent at the Battle of Anegawa.

works.name: Battle: Battle of Okehazama (1560)
works.description: In his breakout early victory, Nobunaga defeated the much larger invading army of Imagawa Yoshimoto through a surprise attack exploiting a thunderstorm, an upset that let him ally with the future Tokugawa Ieyasu.

clues.clue: Exploited a thunderstorm/rainstorm to defeat the much larger army of Imagawa Yoshimoto, hiding troops in a forest (and, in one telling, using fake straw soldiers) while the enemy celebrated
examples: In one early victory, a thunderstorm helped him defeat the much larger force of Imagawa Yoshimoto at Okehazama.
this commander and two thousand men hid in a forest during a rainstorm while creating a group of fake soldiers made of straw to confuse the enemy, who were celebrating their commander's birthday.

clues.clue: Allied with Matsudaira Motoyasu (the future Tokugawa Ieyasu) after the victory at Okehazama
examples: This man allied himself with Matsudaira Motoyasu after defeating Imagawa Yoshimoto at the Battle of Okehazama.

works.name: Event: Siege of Mount Hiei and War on the Ikko-ikki
works.description: Nobunaga waged a sustained campaign against Buddhist militant power, burning the Enryaku-ji temple complex on Mount Hiei and its warrior-monks, destroying an Ikko-ikki stronghold at Nagashima, and finally forcing the surrender of the Ikko-ikki's main fortress at Ishiyama Hongan-ji after a decade-long siege.

clues.clue: Destroyed the Buddhist temple of Enryaku-ji during his siege of Mount Hiei, massacring the sohei warrior monks who opposed him
examples: The Buddhist temple of Enryaku-ji was destroyed by the orders of this man.
This man destroyed the temple of Enryaku-ji during his siege of Mount Hiei, reducing the power of the sohei, or warrior monks, who opposed him.

clues.clue: Sieged the Ikko-ikki's fortress at Ishiyama Hongan-ji in Osaka for roughly a decade, eventually forcing its surrender
examples: This general spent ten years sieging Ishiyama Hongan-ji in order to subdue the Amida Buddha-worshipping Ikko-ikki.
This leader achieved victory over one group by forcing the surrender of Hongan-ji in Osaka.

clues.clue: Destroyed an Ikko-ikki stronghold at Nagashima as revenge
examples: This man took revenge on the ikko-ikki by destroying their stronghold at Nagashima.

clues.clue: Was defeated by Uesugi Kenshin at the Battle of Tedorigawa while attempting to seize Echigo province
examples: This leader's attempt to seize Echigo province was halted at the Battle of Tedorigawa by Uesugi Kenshin.
At the Battle of Tedorigawa, Uesugi inflicted a crushing defeat on this general, the first of the three unifiers of Japan.

works.name: Battle: Battle of Nagashino (1575)
works.description: Nobunaga's most famous victory: at Nagashino he revolutionized Japanese warfare by arranging his arquebusier gunmen behind wooden palisades in three ranks so that rotating volleys were never delayed by reloading, using the tactic (with allied Tokugawa forces) to shatter the cavalry charges of the Takeda clan under Takeda Katsuyori.

clues.clue: Arranged arquebusier gunmen behind wooden palisades in three ranks/rows so rotating volleys would not be delayed by reloading, using the tactic to destroy the Takeda clan's cavalry charges under Takeda Katsuyori
examples: His innovative military techniques included setting up his gunmen behind a wooden palisade in three ranks, so that their volleys would not be delayed by reloading.
In his most famous battle, this shrewd tactician revolutionized his country's warfare when he defeated the Takeda clan by organizing his arquebusiers in three ranks at Nagashino.

clues.clue: Used imported Portuguese arquebuses (tanegashima guns) at Nagashino
examples: At the battle of Nagashino, this general repeatedly repulsed the Takeda Clan's cavalry charges using imported Portuguese arquebuses.

clues.clue: Called this his greatest/most famous victory; allied with Tokugawa Ieyasu at Nagashino after having supported and then turned against the last Ashikaga shogun, Ashikaga Yoshiaki
examples: The Battle of Nagashino may be this man's greatest victory. He supported but later turned against Ashikaga Yoshiaki, the last Ashikaga shogun, and allied with Tokugawa Ieyasu at Nagashino.

works.name: Governance and Castles
works.description: Beyond the battlefield, Nobunaga liberalized his territory's economy, built and named several castles that became seats of power (Gifu and Azuchi), and deposed the last Ashikaga shogun after using him as a puppet ruler.

clues.clue: Broke guild monopolies and liberalized business with his rakuichi rakuza ("free markets, free guilds") policy
examples: This leader broke guild monopolies and liberalized business with his rakuichi rakuza policy and allowed the construction of the Nanban temple in Kyoto.

clues.clue: Allowed construction of the Nanban ("southern barbarian") temple in Kyoto
examples: This leader broke guild monopolies and liberalized business with his rakuichi rakuza policy and allowed the construction of the Nanban temple in Kyoto.

clues.clue: After a siege that captured a mountaintop castle, renamed the castle, the nearby village, and the province all "Gifu"
examples: After leading a siege which ended in the capture of a mountaintop castle, this man renamed the castle, the nearby village, and the province, naming them all Gifu.

clues.clue: Built the first hirayamajiro ("flatland-mountain castle"), at Azuchi, which became his base
examples: This man built the first hirayamajiro, or flatland-mountain castle, at Azuchi.
This daimyo, whose base was at Azuchi Castle, used the personal motto tenka fubu, or "all the world by force of arms."

clues.clue: Installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as a puppet shogun before deposing him in 1573, ending the Ashikaga shogunate
examples: This daimyo deposed Yoshiaki, the last Ashikaga shogun.
He placed one family's Yoshiaki on the throne as his puppet before deposing him in 1573.

clues.clue: Used a personal seal/motto reading "tenka fubu," translated as "all the world by (the) force of arms" or "under the sky"
examples: This man's seal contained the slogan "all the world by the force of arms".
This man used a seal that literally said "under the sky," representing his greatest ambition.

works.name: Foreign Contacts and Religion
works.description: Despite his own irreligion, Nobunaga tolerated and even encouraged the spread of Christianity in Japan through contact with Jesuit missionaries, while his campaigns against rival clans produced gruesome trophies.

clues.clue: Though an atheist, helped establish the first Christian church in Japan
examples: Although this man was an atheist, he helped establish the first church in Japan, and obtained a black slave that he called Yasuke from Jesuit missionaries.

clues.clue: Obtained a black slave from Jesuit missionaries, whom he named Yasuke
examples: Although this man was an atheist, he helped establish the first church in Japan, and obtained a black slave that he called Yasuke from Jesuit missionaries.

clues.clue: After besieging the Azai and Asakura clan castles of Odani and Ichijodani, reportedly had some of his enemies' skulls made into cups for drinking sake
examples: After besieging the castles of Odani and Ichijodani, this man supposedly made some of his enemies' skulls into cups for drinking sake.

works.name: Event: Honno-ji Incident (1582)
works.description: Nobunaga's career and unification drive ended when his own general Akechi Mitsuhide betrayed him and attacked him at the Honno-ji temple in Kyoto, forcing him (and his son) to commit seppuku; he was avenged and succeeded as unifier by his general Toyotomi Hideyoshi.

clues.clue: Was betrayed and attacked by his own retainer/general Akechi Mitsuhide at Honno-ji temple in Kyoto, forcing him to commit seppuku
examples: This man was forced to commit suicide after Akechi Mitsuhide betrayed him in the Honno-ji incident.
This man's death occurred during an incident in Honno-ji, where he was betrayed by Mitsuhide Akechi and committed seppuku.

clues.clue: His son also died alongside him at Honno-ji
examples: This ruler of Owari Province and his son both died at the Incident at Honno-Ji, in which this man was forced to commit suicide by Akechi Mitsuhide.

clues.clue: Was avenged and succeeded as unifier by his general Toyotomi Hideyoshi
examples: After his death in the Honno-ji Incident, this leader was avenged and succeeded by Toyotomi Hideyoshi.
This victor at Nagashino and Okehazama was succeeded by his sandalbearer, Toyotomi Hideyoshi.

works.name: Legacy and Cultural Depictions
works.description: Nobunaga's role as the first of the Three Great Unifiers is memorialized in a famous Japanese poem/idiom comparing the trio's roles in unification through the metaphor of making a rice cake.

clues.clue: A Japanese poem/idiom holds that "Nobunaga pounds the rice [cake], Hideyoshi bakes the cake, and Tokugawa [Ieyasu] eats it," summarizing the three unifiers' roles
examples: In a Japanese idiom, this man "pounds the national rice cake."
A Japanese poem says that "[this man] pounded the rice / Hideyoshi baked the cake / and Tokugawa ate it."

comprehensive_summary: Oda Nobunaga, son of Nobuhide, was initially disfavored in the succession struggle for control of the Oda clan of Owari Province because of his poor conduct — he dressed badly and threw incense at his father's funeral, prompting a retainer's suicide. He defeated his brother Nobuyuki at the Battle of Ino but was persuaded by their mother to pardon him, only to later fake illness and have Nobuyuki killed at Kiyosu Castle on information from the retainer Shibata Katsuie; he was also known for ruthless tactics, such as once eliminating a rival warlord via an assassin hidden in the rival's latrine, and he nearly died himself when an Azai clan samurai charged his tent at the Battle of Anegawa. His breakout victory came at the Battle of Okehazama in 1560, where he exploited a thunderstorm to defeat the much larger invading army of Imagawa Yoshimoto, afterward allying with Matsudaira Motoyasu, the future Tokugawa Ieyasu. Nobunaga then waged a sustained campaign against Buddhist militant power: he destroyed the Enryaku-ji temple complex and its sohei warrior monks during the siege of Mount Hiei, razed an Ikko-ikki stronghold at Nagashima in revenge, and after roughly a decade of siege forced the surrender of the Ikko-ikki's main fortress at Ishiyama Hongan-ji in Osaka — though he was himself defeated by Uesugi Kenshin at the Battle of Tedorigawa while trying to seize Echigo province. His most famous victory came at the 1575 Battle of Nagashino, where he arranged arquebusier gunmen behind wooden palisades in three rotating ranks so that volleys were never delayed by reloading, using imported Portuguese arquebuses to shatter the cavalry charges of the Takeda clan under Takeda Katsuyori; he fought there after having supported and then turned against the last Ashikaga shogun, Ashikaga Yoshiaki, whom he had installed as a puppet before formally deposing him in 1573. As a ruler, Nobunaga broke guild monopolies and liberalized commerce through his rakuichi rakuza policy, allowed construction of the Nanban temple in Kyoto, renamed a captured mountaintop castle (and its village and province) "Gifu," and built the first hirayamajiro, or flatland-mountain castle, at Azuchi, which became his base; he used a personal seal reading "tenka fubu," rendered as either "all the world by force of arms" or "under the sky." Though an atheist, he helped establish the first Christian church in Japan through contact with Jesuit missionaries, from whom he obtained a black slave he named Yasuke, and after besieging the Azai and Asakura clan castles of Odani and Ichijodani he reportedly had some of his enemies' skulls made into sake cups. His career ended in 1582 when his own general Akechi Mitsuhide betrayed him, attacking him at Honno-ji temple in Kyoto and forcing him — along with his son — to commit seppuku in the Honno-ji Incident; he was avenged and succeeded as unifier by his general Toyotomi Hideyoshi. A Japanese poem memorializes the roles of the three unifiers: "Nobunaga pounds the rice [cake], Hideyoshi bakes the cake, and Tokugawa eats it."
recursive_suggestions: Battle of Nagashino — has independent bonus-part answerline presence (3x) distinct from Nobunaga himself; candidate for its own standalone topic
Honno-ji Incident — has independent bonus-part answerline presence (3x) distinct from Nobunaga; candidate for its own topic covering Akechi Mitsuhide's betrayal and motives
Toyotomi Hideyoshi — Nobunaga's successor and general; recurring figure with clear independent answerline presence
Tokugawa Ieyasu — third of the Three Great Unifiers, allied with Nobunaga at Okehazama and Nagashino; independent topic
Akechi Mitsuhide — Nobunaga's betrayer at Honno-ji; watch for confusion since he is sometimes the intended answer rather than Nobunaga
Uesugi Kenshin — the "Dragon of Echigo," defeated Nobunaga at Tedorigawa; has independent bonus answerline presence
Takeda Shingen / Takeda clan — recurring rival power whose death (and later his son Takeda Katsuyori's defeat at Nagashino) benefited Nobunaga; independent answerline
Osaka Castle — has independent bonus-part answerline presence; built by Hideyoshi on the site of the former Ishiyama Hongan-ji fortress
Ikko-ikki — recurring religious-militant answerline (2x) with independent bonus presence, distinct from Nobunaga's campaigns against them
